speaker: interviewer
text: Are you doing the slate [INAUDIBLE]? Oh, [INAUDIBLE] do that?

speaker: interviewer
text: This is round one with Fred Buch, round one, Fred Buch. Today's date, December 22, 1994. The testimony of Fred Buch, interviewer Norman Pell, city Los Angeles, language English. Mr. Buch, tell us your full name, your date of birth, and where you were born.

speaker: interviewee
text: Fred, Fred, before I was Fritz. Now, I am Fred Buch, born in December 13, 1900 in Wiener Neustadt in Austria. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] That is all.

speaker: interviewer
text: What kind of a city was Wiener Neustadt? What--

speaker: interviewee
text: It was a city, maybe-- it is-- it is an industrial city and on the foot of the Alps. It's a nice city. And it was-- a military academy was there. That is all that I know from when I was there. [BACKGROUND NOISE] I left Wiener Neustadt when I was nine years old.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have any brothers and sisters?

speaker: interviewee
text: One brother. One brother, I have.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was her older than you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, [BACKGROUND NOISE] two years older. And he-- yeah, we grew up both in Wiener Neustadt.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did-- what did your father do?

speaker: interviewee
text: He was over inspector of the railroad company Sudbahn railroad, state-- state railway.

speaker: interviewer
text: And he was a supervisor with the railroad company.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. Over inspector, he was there. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: Did he travel?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. He didn't travel. Only he-- we-- he was assigned to [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] to-- to be superintendent of a railroad station where he had-- he was the-- the-- the conductor of the-- of the railroad station. [BACKGROUND NOISE] So we came in and out of town. We had to-- that was in 1910. We went to Atzgersdorf Mauer, it's called, a city maybe 6 miles from-- from Vienna, closer to Vienna. [BACKGROUND NOISE] Wiener Neustadt was maybe 25 miles or 30 miles away from Vienna. So we came closer to Vienna.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember what the Jewish community was like in Wiener Neustadt?

speaker: interviewee
text: As a matter of fact, I know only of one Jewish family my parents were befriended. That-- yeah. In Wiener Neustadt, one Jewish family. And in Atzgersdorf, also one Jewish family. So there were not many Jews there, I believe so. And, yeah, from the school-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] in-- in-- in school, I didn't meet very-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] maybe none. None of the children were Jewish. Especially in-- in Atzgersdorf Mauer, they were quite anti-Semitic. The-- the children, they beat me up, because I was-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I was crucifying Jesus Christs.

speaker: interviewer
text: So that-- that was the old idea, the old anti-Semitism. But there were not many-- many Jews. So we really had not very much Jewish connection, upbringing. Upbringing was not Jewish family. And as you remember, you were the only Jewish boy in your class?

speaker: interviewee
text: I believe so. Yeah. Yeah. I believe so. As a matter of fact, in Wiener Neustadt, we went to a private school. It was in-- in the-- a Protestant school, a very good school. I do not know was it because the parents wanted to protect us from the influence of the mostly Catholic surrounding, which was mostly also anti-Semitic, but not the Protestant schools. It was-- we had a very nice, very nice upbringing in this school in Wiener Neustadt. Then in Atzgersdorf, that it was already hard. There what where I had physical-- I had to suffer of being a Jewish kid. Even the-- the teacher made-- made remarks. It was unbelievable. And there was no-- there was no help, legal help, so you couldn't do it. My father went once to the director of the-- of the school and complained to very little avail. It didn't help him very much. And the remarks that the teacher made about-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] eh, that was the upbringing in the old country, in a very, very religious surrounding.

speaker: interviewer
text: In your own home, did you--

speaker: interviewee
text: Pardon me?

speaker: interviewer
text: In your own home, did you observe any of the Jewish holidays? Shabbat?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. It was quite an assimilate surrounding. Although, we, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] maybe due to the circumstances, due to the surrounding, we had to know that we are Jewish. And we didn't hide it. But there was no-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] yeah. There was-- there were Yom Kippur, Rosh Hashanah. But there was no ceremony in the house, no-- no nothing.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me, what was a normal day like?

speaker: interviewee
text: The normal what?

speaker: interviewer
text: A normal day, a normal week? You went to school?

speaker: interviewee
text: You went to school, came home, made homework, and had to practice the-- the-- the violin, had to-- yeah. We were lucky. You see, we had the season change. We have rain and snow.

speaker: interviewer
text: So what is the kid to do? You cannot play outside like California. You stayed at home. And what shall you do? You read a book. And that was a blessing. So we had-- yeah. It was reading and talking with mother. Mother was a very intelligent woman and quite progressive. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Because she came from Lwow. And there was, in these days, there was always a Polish resistance against the Austrian monarchy. So she-- she became a Polish nationalist, I would say, but a socialist. You know? So she was very progressive. And it-- it was pleasant to be with her.

speaker: interviewee
text: Father was always at work. We were with the mother. And she had a very beneficial influence on us that-- as children. We grew up. Then when we got older and had to go to the university, and--

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, you lived in that same town, grew up--

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no, no. Then-- then from Atzgersdorf Mauer, we went to Modling. And Modling is a-- it's not-- [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] it's a nationalistic city. You see, Wiener Neustadt was a Catholic religious city. Modling was a nationalist city. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] It was better under-- under nationalist-inclined surrounding. They are not so bigots. So it was-- it was better there.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how old were you when you moved to Modling.

speaker: interviewee
text: 14. 14, yeah. So the milieu changed drastically. And we felt much, much better off. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] It was-- especially it was this-- the World War I started in 1914, so you have to be patriotic, super patriotic. And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] yeah, it-- it was-- it was better.

speaker: interviewer
text: And tell me about the Jewish community in Modling, was there--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. There was-- there was a big Jewish community. And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I know to the holidays, we went there. We all, we all youngsters, we went there not-- not for the service, for the-- to be-- to be among us, to be among young children, 14, 15, 16 years old.

speaker: interviewer
text: So there were boys and girls. It was always interesting and an amusing event. The Jewish holidays, we went there. There was a big hall. We didn't spend much time in the-- in the synagogue. And how long did you live in Modling?

speaker: interviewee
text: I want to say about-- we had-- we had to be-- we had to have religious [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] instructions by law, the Catholics by a priest and the Jews by-- by a rabbi.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how was that? The rabbi came to the house?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. No, no, no. We had to go Sundays. Sundays we had to go to the synagogue. And he had there a room. And he-- he taught. And it was awful. It was awful. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] There was a [NON-ENGLISH], you know, the [NON-ENGLISH]. On the left side, it was German. And on the right side, it was in Hebrew.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we had to read. We had to cover up the German, the translation. And we had to read the-- it was the Bible. But we didn't understand.

speaker: interviewee
text: We could read. But we didn't understand. And-- but the rabbi, he wanted put your hand on the-- on the German text. Don't look what you read. You tell me what-- [LAUGHS] but he was a doctor of philosophy and theology, an interesting-- interesting man. And I sort of got interested in the [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] religious philosophy, but not what he was teaching. So I wanted to have more [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] explanation of the Bible. And I went to him and said, I would like to-- to have private talks with him in his studio at home. [BACKGROUND NOISE] And he didn't give me. And that was a big disappointment. That this was-- he did not understand, really, what young people wanted. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] We were not believers. We were not believers. No, no. But-- but still we knew there is some Jewish philosophy. And that was the Jewish philosopher. We heard about the Jewish philosopher.

speaker: interviewer
text: So that was the extent of your [NON-ENGLISH] Hebrew learning.

speaker: interviewee
text: Right. Right. Then started the Zionistic movement. Yeah. And there my best friend, my best friend, he was 16 years old. He called about the assistance of the-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] [NON-ENGLISH]. [NON-ENGLISH], he's a famous man in Vienna. He was representative in the parliament. And he called him to- to help him to organize a Zionistic group in Modling. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] But he was a young-- he was too young really, to guide it. And he was-- very-- became very chauvinistic, nationalistic chauvinistic. And I was from the other side, you know. As I said, my mother, she was a socialist. I was for that. So we didn't-- and he was my best friend. And we got into trouble. We didn't talk to each other for-- for a year, only for political reason. But when I needed an affidavit, he was before I came to the States. When I needed an affidavit to get the American visa, he was there. And he helped me. And it was a very interesting time.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did he do for the-- to get money for the Zionistic group? He put on the he put a-- a list on the doors of the synagogue how much everybody gave. And he made a mark that that guy, he gave only-- only so-- and I said, that is not right. [LAUGHS] [INAUDIBLE] things-- So by this time, you were a teenager. Did you meet your wife in Modling?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. No. I met my wife--

speaker: interviewer
text: How long were you in Modling?

speaker: interviewee
text: Ah, from-- from [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] 1913 till 1928, only 15 years. Is that 15? Yeah. So, yeah, in Modling. My wife I met after I came back from Egypt. I went to Egypt.

speaker: interviewer
text: So while you were in Modling, you were there for those 15 years. Was there any military service or--

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yeah. I had-- I had to-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I was drafted [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] for the Austrian army in 19-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] [INAUDIBLE]. 1917. I was drafted in 1917, prepared to start my military service in-- February, 1918.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I was really 17 years old. Because I am born on December 13, 1900. And the law said, all people born in 1900 have to serve. And so I was-- I was one of the last men in the-- in 1900, but one of the first men of the 20th century. So I had to serve in the Austrian army. And what did you do in the army?

speaker: interviewee
text: What I remember, I was in the army. First of all, I had-- because I had gymnasium, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I had the privilege after the gymnasium, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] the-- the education permitted me to go in the officers' school for the infantry. But I had to, still, to go to that what they, the-- the non-officer [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] soldiers has to do.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I had to-- for instance, I had to stay guard on the poppy-- on the poppy field. I know there was a huge-- that was in the Ukraine-- a huge poppy field. And I had to guard there and watch that-- that nobody takes the poppies off. I can only imagine that maybe the military needed the poppy for medical purposes.

speaker: interviewee
text: A huge poppy field-- so that was my-- my activity in the-- yeah. And education for-- for being an officer, which I didn't want to. I was absolutely-- I prepared myself to flank the officer. Because I did not want to be an officer. If I have to be a soldier, I want to be with the-- with the people, with the-- with the-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] with the ordinary soldiers.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I was several months in the-- in the infantry officer's school. Then when- when the war in Italy became hot, they-- the army needed officers for artillery, artillery and horses. There was the question, who wants to go in the-- in the-- officers school for artillery? Oh, here I was.

speaker: interviewee
text: I didn't want to go to war. I didn't want to shoot anybody. So I-- I was lucky. They sent me-- from the Ukraine, they sent me to Vienna, to the officer's school. So there, where I was at home. I was very happy. And then came--

speaker: interviewer
text: How long--

speaker: interviewee
text: --came the revolution, and we-- we chased the-- the monarch out of his power. And, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: So how long were you in the army?

speaker: interviewee
text: From February till October. In October, we revolted. And we-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I was in the army. That is why we lost the war, I tell you. [LAUGHTER] I was a poor soldier. Didn't like it.

speaker: interviewer
text: After the army, then what happened?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. Then started my education. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I started engineering, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] construction engineering. And that took four years, until I got my diploma. Then the-- when the war was over, there was a big misery in Austria. One couldn't-- I couldn't find a job.

speaker: interviewee
text: So students, Arab students, with whom I started together in Vienna, they came, many, from the Arab countries. And I had a friend. I had two friends there.

speaker: interviewee
text: And they said, come with us. We are going home. Come with us to Egypt. You will find a job. So I went. It was-- I was 24 years old.

speaker: interviewer
text: And that was the first time you had left home?

speaker: interviewee
text: The first time that I left home, and just a few-- a few dollars, or schillings. No, what was it then? Krones-- a few krones in my hand. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] My father gave me little money, very little money. There was not much money in the house.

speaker: interviewee
text: So to look for my for-- for my existence in a strange land, no language, nothing. I spoke a little French that I learned in school and [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] came there. And the friend went their own way. And we had-- I had very little. Because they lived in their [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Arab quarters, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Arab habits and so.

speaker: interviewee
text: But I didn't fit there. So we were separated. And I found a-- found a job. And Morpuro was his name, a very nice man, Italian Jew. He gave me a job helping his bookkeeper. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I took-- I took a course in bookkeeping in Vienna just to be prepared for-- for business eventually. That helped me. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Yeah. There I was a few months. Then [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] I got a job with a huge German company in Suez, Holzmann. It's a very famous company. I was very happy about it. And that was-- [PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS] that was in 1925. 1925, I got the job. But do not forget. Then, already when I started engineering, I had to cope with [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] with the new Nazi movement, which started in 1920, 1921. And we Jewish students, we had to-- to fight them. You know, they started to use the university for their Nazi propaganda, the swastikas, on the-- on the-- in the lecture hall. And we had-- we had to do something against it. So we had quite the trouble with them. And back to Egypt, when I was with Holzmann, I was very happy to have a nice position. And there came an influx from Germany. They came, young engineers, young work-- working men, all already with the Nazi spirit. And they made my life miserable there. And so I quit. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: How did they make your life miserable?

speaker: interviewee
text: In-- the-- in the work, you see. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] We were we were-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] in the desert surveying. I had to survey. And I needed their assistance. We had to cooperate. And they-- with purpose, they mixed up so that I shall come out with-- with results which did not-- did not fit the reality. So surveying is a very delicate thing. And, if the-- the workers and the helpers want to to-- boycott, to obstruct, they can do it very easy.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I gave up. And I went to Port Said. And there I found also a nice job. The funny thing is that two of the-- of the people who were working with me in Suez in Holzmann, they came-- they were people who were holding the-- how do you-- you know what the-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] there is a stick to make the measurements. The surveying equipment.

speaker: interviewee
text: Surveying, survey equipment--
